issue_title: Agents App: Session List Visual Improvements
issue_body:
# Session List Visual Improvements

## Overview
The session list needs better visual hierarchy, improved readability (especially in light theme), and more engaging feedback for in-progress sessions.

**Related Files:**
- `src/vs/sessions/contrib/sessions/browser/views/sessionsList.ts`
- `src/vs/sessions/contrib/sessions/browser/media/sessionsList.css`

## Issues & Opportunities

### 1. Color Contrast (Light Theme Priority)
**Problem:** Text contrast between session titles and timestamps is too low, making the list difficult to scan.

**Potential Solutions:**
- [ ] Increase contrast ratio for secondary text (timestamps) in light theme
- [ ] Audit all text colors against WCAG AA standards (4.5:1 for normal text)
- [ ] Ensure session titles stand out as the primary element
- [ ] Consider subtle weight differences (medium vs. regular) to establish hierarchy

**Current Implementation:**
```typescript
// From sessionsList.ts
private static readonly ITEM_HEIGHT = 54;
```
Each item has title + metadata (time), but contrast may need adjustment.

### 2. Visual Noise from Separator Dots
**Problem:** Dots between session type icon and text create visual clutter.

**Potential Solutions:**
- [ ] Remove separator dots entirely
- [ ] Test spacing alone to separate icon from text
- [ ] Consider alternative subtle separators if needed (light vertical line, margin only, etc.)
- [ ] Validate with various session types and icon combinations

### 3. In-Progress Session Indicator Animation
**Problem:** Current in-progress icon feels static for a "pro-sumer" product; missed opportunity for engaging micro-interaction.

**Potential Solutions:**
- [ ] Add subtle animation/motion to in-progress indicator
- [ ] Options to explore:
  - Gentle pulsing (fade in/out)
  - Subtle rotation or shimmer
  - Breathing animation (scale or opacity)
  - Gradient shift
- [ ] Ensure animation is:
  - Subtle enough not to distract
  - Respectful of motion preferences (`prefers-reduced-motion`)
  - Performance-friendly (CSS animations, not JS)

### 4. Overall List Scannability
**Problem:** Items may not differentiate enough visually to enable quick scanning.

**Potential Solutions:**
- [ ] Ensure adequate vertical spacing between items
- [ ] Consider hover states that feel responsive and polished
- [ ] Add subtle dividers or background treatments for separation if needed

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Session list is easy to scan at a glance
- [ ] Color contrast meets or exceeds WCAG AA standards
- [ ] Visual hierarchy clearly distinguishes titles from metadata
- [ ] In-progress sessions feel dynamic without being distracting
- [ ] Overall aesthetic feels modern and "pro-sumer" quality

## Design Notes
- We can be slightly more expressive than traditional developer tools
- Motion should enhance the experience, not distract from it
- Always respect user accessibility preferences (reduced motion, high contrast)

## Implementation Considerations
```css
/* Example animation approach for in-progress icon */
@keyframes session-in-progress {
  0%, 100% { opacity: 1; }
  50% { opacity: 0.6; }
}

.session-in-progress-icon {
  animation: session-in-progress 2s ease-in-out infinite;
}

@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {
  .session-in-progress-icon {
    animation: none;
  }
}
```
pr_title: sessions: add pinned sidebar section
pr_body: ## Summary Related to https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/310018 - render pinned chats under a dedicated `Pinned` section header in the sessions sidebar - reuse the existing section-header presentation so the label stays uppercase like workspace and folder headers - keep the pinned section toolbar limited to `Mark All as Done`, without workspace-specific actions like `New Session` - add grouping tests and update the sessions layout spec ## Notes - compile-check and hygiene passed in this environment - the targeted Electron-backed unit test bootstrap failed in this CLI environment before reaching the sessions code (`app.setPath` on an undefined Electron app)

issue_title: kubelet TopologyManager Stalls on Large NUMA Systems (e.g. Nvidia GB200 Compute Tray with 34 NUMA nodes)
issue_body:
### What happened?

When scheduling a pod (e.g. KubeVirt virt-launcher pod) that requests a PCI device on a very large NUMA system, kubelet’s TopologyManager never finishes computing topology hints and the pod is effectively stuck in admission:

- The pod is scheduled to the node.
- kubelet never reaches the device Allocate call for the requested resource.
- From a higher-level consumer (KubeVirt in our case), the virt-launcher pod stays in Pending and the VM remains in Starting with Ready = False.
- kubelet is responsive, but many goroutines are stuck in topology hint generation.

In our environment this manifests as:

- KubeVirt VM with a hostDevice (GPU, InfiniBand or NVMe) never transitions to Running, even though the node has the capacity and the device is free.
- Disabling TopologyManager or constraining the NUMA fan-out immediately lets the VM start

### What did you expect to happen?

kubelet should compute topology hints in bounded time, even on systems with many NUMA nodes (in a case GB200 with 34 NUMA nodes where only 2 NUMA nodes have CPUs, the rest are for HBM/NVLink), and call device-plugin Allocate so that pods/VMs using PCI host devices can start.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

## High-level repro:
1. Use a host with a large NUMA topology  
    Example: Nvidia GB200-class system with 34 NUMA nodes visible to the OS.
 
2. Run Kubernetes with kubelet configured roughly as follows:
```yaml
kubeReserved:
  cpu: "2"
  memory: 4Gi
  ephemeral-storage: 20Gi
systemReserved:
  cpu: "2"
  memory: 8Gi
  ephemeral-storage: 20Gi
topologyManagerPolicy: best-effort
topologyManagerScope: pod
topologyManagerPolicyOptions:
  prefer-closest-numa-nodes: "true"
  max-allowable-numa-nodes: "34"
cpuManagerPolicy: static
reservedSystemCPUs: "0-3,72-75"
cpuManagerPolicyOptions:
  distribute-cpus-across-numa: "false"
```
3. Deploy a device plugin that:
- Advertises a PCI resource (e.g. GPU, InfiniBand or NVMe) as a kubelet device resource.
- Exposes the device with topology information referencing many NUMA nodes (up to 34 in our case).

4. Create a Pod
Requests one unit of that device resource.
Uses Guaranteed QoS (requests == limits for CPU/memory) so that TopologyManager participates fully.
 
5. Observe
- The pod is scheduled to the node.
- kubelet never calls Allocate on the device plugin for that resource.
- The pod stays in Pending indefinitely.
- KubeVirt-specific repro (how we hit it)
- Instead of a raw pod, create a KubeVirt VirtualMachineInstance that uses hostDevices:
```yaml
devices:
   hostDevices:
    - deviceName: nvidia.com/GB100_HGX_GB200
      name: gpu1
```

pr_body:
## What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

## What this PR does / why we need it

On systems with many NUMA nodes (e.g. NVIDIA GB200 compute trays with 36 OS-visible NUMA nodes, only 2 CPU NUMA nodes), the device manager's `generateDeviceTopologyHints` calls `bitmask.IterateBitMasks` over **all** kubelet-known NUMA nodes. Since `IterateBitMasks` enumerates every non-empty subset, the cost is O(2^n) — with n=34 that is ~17 billion iterations. This causes kubelet to stall indefinitely during topology hint computation and the pod never reaches `Allocate`.

### Root cause
The iteration space is derived from `m.numaNodes`, which includes every NUMA node the OS exposes. On GB200 the majority of those nodes carry only GPU HBM (Coherent Device Memory) and host zero devices for any given
device-plugin resource. Including them inflates n without contributing useful hints.

This PR constrains the bitmask iteration to only the NUMA nodes that actually host available or reusable devices for the requested resource, reducing the search space from 2^n (machine-wide) to 2^k (device-relevant), where k is typically 1–2 on GB200 systems.

### Fix

- Introduce `deviceNUMANodes(resource)`, which collects the NUMA node IDs from **all registered devices** (`m.allDevices[resource]`) regardless of allocation state. `generateDeviceTopologyHints` now iterates only over
those nodes, reducing the search space from 2^n (machine-wide) to 2^k (device-relevant), where k is typically 1–2 on GB200 systems.

- Using `allDevices` (rather than `available ∪ reusable`) ensures that `minAffinitySize` and `Preferred` flags are computed identically to the original code — device-less NUMA nodes contribute zero to `devicesInMask`
and can never reduce `minAffinitySize`, so any mask containing them is always non-preferred. Removing them from iteration is therefore **behavior-preserving** for all existing configurations.

### Changes

1. **`deviceNUMANodes(resource)`** — new helper that returns the sorted list of NUMA node IDs hosting at least one device for the given resource. Falls back to `m.numaNodes` when no topology info exists.

2. **`generateDeviceTopologyHints()`** — now calls `IterateBitMasks(deviceNUMANodes, …)` instead of `IterateBitMasks(m.numaNodes, …)`. No synthetic fallback hint is needed because the iteration already covers every NUMA node that could satisfy a request.

3. **Unit tests** :     
   - `TestDeviceNUMANodes`: validates NUMA node collection from `allDevices` and fallback behavior.
   - `TestGenerateDeviceTopologyHintsFiltersNUMANodes`: verifies that device-less NUMA nodes are excluded from hints, including a 34-device-less-node scenario.
   - `TestFilteredDeviceHintsMergeWithOtherProviders`: confirms that filtered device hints merge correctly with CPU/memory providers through the TopologyManager.

## Which issue(s) this PR fixes

Fixes https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/135541

## How has this been tested?

- All existing `TestGetTopologyHints`, `TestGetPodTopologyHints`, and `TestTopologyAlignedAllocation` tests pass unchanged.
- `TestDeviceNUMANodes` tests validate NUMA filtering and fallback.
- `TestGenerateDeviceTopologyHintsFiltersNUMANodes ` validates hint filtering including a 34-device-less-node scenario.
- `TestFilteredDeviceHintsMergeWithOtherProviders` validates cross-provider hint merging through TopologyManager.
- Verified with `hack/verify-golangci-lint.sh pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/...`: 0 lint issues.

### Validation on NVIDIA GB200 bare-metal node

Tested on a GB200 compute tray (36 NUMA nodes) with the following kubelet configuration:
​
```yaml
cpuManagerPolicyOptions:
  distribute-cpus-across-numa: "true"
topologyManagerPolicy: best-effort
topologyManagerScope: pod
topologyManagerPolicyOptions:
  prefer-closest-numa-nodes: "true"
  max-allowable-numa-nodes: "36"
```

Created a KubeVirt VMI with `dedicatedCpuPlacement`, 120 vCPUs, 4 Blackwell GPU passthrough devices, and EGM or hugepages. With this fix applied, the VMI reaches `Running` state and `Ready=True`:
​
```
$ kubectl get vmi -owide
NAME                         AGE   PHASE     IP            NODENAME     READY   LIVE-MIGRATABLE   PAUSED
test-120cpu-numa-4gpu-vm   29m   Running   10.244.3.17   computetray-w8   True    False
```

Without this fix, the same virt-launcher pod would remain stuck in `Pending` indefinitely due to the topology hint computation stall described in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/135541.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

release-note:
```
Fixed a kubelet device manager bug where topology hint computation enumerated O(2^n) NUMA node combinations using all machine NUMA nodes. On systems with many NUMA nodes that carry no devices (e.g. NVIDIA GB200 with 36 NUMA nodes), this caused kubelet to stall indefinitely during pod admission. The device manager now restricts iteration to NUMA nodes that actually host devices for the requested resource, reducing the search space to O(2^k) where k is typically 1–2.
```

kubelet TopologyManager Stalls on Large NUMA Systems (e.g. Nvidia GB200 Compute Tray with 34 NUMA nodes) ### What happened?

When scheduling a pod (e.g. KubeVirt virt-launcher pod) that requests a PCI device on a very large NUMA system, kubelet’s TopologyManager never finishes computing topology hints and the pod is effectively stuck in admission:

- The pod is scheduled to the node.
- kubelet never reaches the device Allocate call for the requested resource.
- From a higher-level consumer (KubeVirt in our case), the virt-launcher pod stays in Pending and the VM remains in Starting with Ready = False.
- kubelet is responsive, but many goroutines are stuck in topology hint generation.

In our environment this manifests as:

- KubeVirt VM with a hostDevice (GPU, InfiniBand or NVMe) never transitions to Running, even though the node has the capacity and the device is free.
- Disabling TopologyManager or constraining the NUMA fan-out immediately lets the VM start

### What did you expect to happen?

kubelet should compute topology hints in bounded time, even on systems with many NUMA nodes (in a case GB200 with 34 NUMA nodes where only 2 NUMA nodes have CPUs, the rest are for HBM/NVLink), and call device-plugin Allocate so that pods/VMs using PCI host devices can start.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

## High-level repro:
1. Use a host with a large NUMA topology  
    Example: Nvidia GB200-class system with 34 NUMA nodes visible to the OS.
 
2. Run Kubernetes with kubelet configured roughly as follows:
```yaml
kubeReserved:
  cpu: "2"
  memory: 4Gi
  ephemeral-storage: 20Gi
systemReserved:
  cpu: "2"
  memory: 8Gi
  ephemeral-storage: 20Gi
topologyManagerPolicy: best-effort
topologyManagerScope: pod
topologyManagerPolicyOptions:
  prefer-closest-numa-nodes: "true"
  max-allowable-numa-nodes: "34"
cpuManagerPolicy: static
reservedSystemCPUs: "0-3,72-75"
cpuManagerPolicyOptions:
  distribute-cpus-across-numa: "false"
```
3. Deploy a device plugin that:
- Advertises a PCI resource (e.g. GPU, InfiniBand or NVMe) as a kubelet device resource.
- Exposes the device with topology information referencing many NUMA nodes (up to 34 in our case).

4. Create a Pod
Requests one unit of that device resource.
Uses Guaranteed QoS (requests == limits for CPU/memory) so that TopologyManager participates fully.
 
5. Observe
- The pod is scheduled to the node.
- kubelet never calls Allocate on the device plugin for that resource.
- The pod stays in Pending indefinitely.
- KubeVirt-specific repro (how we hit it)
- Instead of a raw pod, create a KubeVirt VirtualMachineInstance that uses hostDevices:
```yaml
devices:
   hostDevices:
    - deviceName: nvidia.com/GB100_HGX_GB200
      name: gpu1
```
The pod (if using KubeVirt, it is virt-launcher pod for a VM) is scheduled to the large NUMA node but stays Pending forever. The pod stuck at Pending, (if using KubeVirt, the VM status remains Starting) and never reaches Ready, despite sufficient resources.


### Anything else we need to know?

## Root cause analysis

- The kubelet TopologyManager asks each hint provider (CPU, memory, device) to compute topology hints over the set of NUMA nodes that the kubelet knows about.
- For the device plugin case, generateDeviceTopologyHints ends up calling bitmask.IterateBitMasks across a bitmask spanning all NUMA IDs known to the machine, not just those relevant to the requested device.
- With 34 NUMA nodes this becomes effectively an exponential search (O(2^n)) over the 34-bit mask.
- On a GB200 host with 34 NUMA nodes and a device plugin that advertises per-node topology across those nodes, generateDeviceTopologyHints spends unbounded time iterating combinations that are not actually schedulable for the requested device.
- While TopologyManager is doing this, kubelet never reaches the device Allocate call, so pods/VMs that need that device are permanently stuck in admission.

## Evidence observed

- Goroutine dumps from kubelet show many workers blocked under TopologyManager/device-manager paths, repeatedly calling IterateBitMasks on a large bitmask.
- No calls to Allocate are observed for the affected device resource while the system is in this hung state.
- If we disable TopologyManager, then virt-launcher pods immediately proceed and the KubeVirt VM starts successfully.

### Kubernetes version

```console
$ kubectl version
Client Version: v1.34.2
Kustomize Version: v5.7.1
Server Version: v1.34.2
```

### Cloud provider

Bare metal, GB200 compute tray

### OS version

```console
# On Linux:
$ cat /etc/os-release
PRETTY_NAME="Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS"
NAME="Ubuntu"
VERSION_ID="24.04"

PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/legal/terms-and-policies/privacy-policy"
UBUNTU_CODENAME=noble
LOGO=ubuntu-logo

$ uname -a
Linux GB200-node-4 6.14.0-1013-nvidia-64k #13-Ubuntu SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Wed Oct 29 09:44:30 UTC 2025 aarch64 aarch64 aarch64 GNU/Linux
```

This PR constrains the bitmask iteration to only the NUMA nodes that actually host available or reusable devices for the requested resource, reducing the search space from 2^n (machine-wide) to 2^k (device-relevant), where k is typically 1–2 on GB200 systems.

### Fix

- Introduce `deviceNUMANodes(resource)`, which collects the NUMA node IDs from **all registered devices** (`m.allDevices[resource]`) regardless of allocation state. `generateDeviceTopologyHints` now iterates only over
those nodes, reducing the search space from 2^n (machine-wide) to 2^k (device-relevant), where k is typically 1–2 on GB200 systems.

- Using `allDevices` (rather than `available ∪ reusable`) ensures that `minAffinitySize` and `Preferred` flags are computed identically to the original code — device-less NUMA nodes contribute zero to `devicesInMask`
and can never reduce `minAffinitySize`, so any mask containing them is always non-preferred. Removing them from iteration is therefore **behavior-preserving** for all existing configurations.

### Changes

1. **`deviceNUMANodes(resource)`** — new helper that returns the sorted list of NUMA node IDs hosting at least one device for the given resource. Falls back to `m.numaNodes` when no topology info exists.

2. **`generateDeviceTopologyHints()`** — now calls `IterateBitMasks(deviceNUMANodes, …)` instead of `IterateBitMasks(m.numaNodes, …)`. No synthetic fallback hint is needed because the iteration already covers every NUMA node that could satisfy a request.

3. **Unit tests** :     
   - `TestDeviceNUMANodes`: validates NUMA node collection from `allDevices` and fallback behavior.
   - `TestGenerateDeviceTopologyHintsFiltersNUMANodes`: verifies that device-less NUMA nodes are excluded from hints, including a 34-device-less-node scenario.
   - `TestFilteredDeviceHintsMergeWithOtherProviders`: confirms that filtered device hints merge correctly with CPU/memory providers through the TopologyManager.

## Which issue(s) this PR fixes

Fixes https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/135541

## How has this been tested?

- All existing `TestGetTopologyHints`, `TestGetPodTopologyHints`, and `TestTopologyAlignedAllocation` tests pass unchanged.
- `TestDeviceNUMANodes` tests validate NUMA filtering and fallback.
- `TestGenerateDeviceTopologyHintsFiltersNUMANodes ` validates hint filtering including a 34-device-less-node scenario.
- `TestFilteredDeviceHintsMergeWithOtherProviders` validates cross-provider hint merging through TopologyManager.
- Verified with `hack/verify-golangci-lint.sh pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/...`: 0 lint issues.

### Validation on NVIDIA GB200 bare-metal node

Tested on a GB200 compute tray (36 NUMA nodes) with the following kubelet configuration:
​
```yaml
cpuManagerPolicyOptions:
  distribute-cpus-across-numa: "true"
topologyManagerPolicy: best-effort
topologyManagerScope: pod
topologyManagerPolicyOptions:
  prefer-closest-numa-nodes: "true"
  max-allowable-numa-nodes: "36"
```

Created a KubeVirt VMI with `dedicatedCpuPlacement`, 120 vCPUs, 4 Blackwell GPU passthrough devices, and EGM or hugepages. With this fix applied, the VMI reaches `Running` state and `Ready=True`:
​
```
$ kubectl get vmi -owide
NAME                         AGE   PHASE     IP            NODENAME     READY   LIVE-MIGRATABLE   PAUSED
test-120cpu-numa-4gpu-vm   29m   Running   10.244.3.17   computetray-w8   True    False
```

Without this fix, the same virt-launcher pod would remain stuck in `Pending` indefinitely due to the topology hint computation stall described in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/135541.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

release-note:
```
Fixed a kubelet device manager bug where topology hint computation enumerated O(2^n) NUMA node combinations using all machine NUMA nodes. On systems with many NUMA nodes that carry no devices (e.g. NVIDIA GB200 with 36 NUMA nodes), this caused kubelet to stall indefinitely during pod admission. The device manager now restricts iteration to NUMA nodes that actually host devices for the requested resource, reducing the search space to O(2^k) where k is typically 1–2.
```

issue_title: kubelet does not start when kubelet folder is on ZFS filesystem because of missing cadvisor plugin in 1.36.0
issue_body:
### What happened?

After upgrading to Kubernetes 1.36.0, the kubelet crashes directly after startup on systems that have the `/var/lib/kubelet` folder configured on a ZFS filesystem. The direct error shown in the logs is:

```
E0423 21:45:14.682963  326484 kubelet.go:1821] "Failed to start ContainerManager" err="failed to get rootfs info: failed to get device for dir \"/var/lib/kubelet\": with major: 0, minor: 60: could not find device in cached partitions map"
```

This error is caused by the kubelet not being able to determine the available space for empheral storage, which it normally gets using cadvisor.

After increasing the log level, it seems that this is caused by cadvisor not having access to its ZFS plugin:
```
Apr 23 21:45:14 strike01 kubelet[326484]: I0423 21:45:14.680361  326484 fs.go:406] no plugin found for filesystem type: zfs
```

### What did you expect to happen?

Like Kubernetes 1.35.3 (or 1.35.4 for that matter): I expect the kubelet to startup and work.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

As described above: Ensure `/var/lib/kubelet` is on a ZFS filesystem and start the kubelet with any configuration.

### Anything else we need to know?

I am aware that Kubernetes does not maintain cadvisor and that cadvisor bugs should not be posted here. However, I believe that cadvisor is working correctly, it is just missing its ZFS plugin.

FS plug-ins in cadvisor are introduced in version v0.55.0, before the introduction everything was built-in. Kubernetes 1.36.0 brings cadvisor from version [v0.53.0 → v0.56.0](https://github.com/google/cadvisor/compare/v0.53.0...v0.56.0). It was specifically introduced in https://github.com/google/cadvisor/commit/28ecaa9d9b4aa2ea878f7692a315797c2a9d7a7c

In the PR merging 0.55.1, #135829, I see that some file system extensions are specifically being imported (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135829/changes#diff-ca41d4fa89358cfbf866e71de1c54ab978a448e805495bf8bca923d85dae31fb), but the ZFS plug-in is missing.

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
Client Version: v1.36.0
Kustomize Version: v5.8.1
Server Version: v1.36.0
```

</details>


### Cloud provider

<details>

N/A, `kubeadm` cluster on bare metal
</details>


### OS version

```

</details>

pr_title: Test that Service DNS uses EndpointSlices
pr_body:
#### What this PR does / why we need it:
Followup to #132019; that confirmed that service proxies use EndpointSlices, but we don't yet confirm that service DNS implementations use EndpointSlices as well.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/4974

#### Special notes for your reviewer:
This is currently expected to fail against some GCE configurations, because they still use kube-dns, which does not support EndpointSlice. There is a plan to deprecate kube-dns and move all CI to CoreDNS. (Once that happens we will add this test to conformance.)

[EDIT] ... except it doesn't fail?

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
```release-note
NONE
```

#### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:
```docs
- [KEP]: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-network/4974-deprecate-endpoints
```

/kind cleanup
/sig network
/triage accepted

issue_title: [declarative validation] `+k8s:minimum` / `+k8s:maximum` do not correctly handle signedness and associated type bounds/boundary-values (ex: int32 vs int64)
issue_body:
Declarative validation's `validation-gen`'s `ParseInt` returns Go `int` (64-bit) but does not validate that the parsed value fits in the target field's type. Values outside the target type's range are silently accepted at codegen time. For unsigned types, large values cannot be parsed at all. No boundary condition tests exist.

### Impact

**int32 vs int64:** All Kubernetes API integers are `int32` or `int64`. Writing `+k8s:minimum=-2147483649` on an `int32` field passes codegen without error but emits a literal that overflows `int32`.

**Unsigned types:** `strconv.Atoi` cannot parse values above `math.MaxInt64`, so `+k8s:maximum=18446744073709551615` on a `uint64` field fails. No kubernets native API types use unsigned integers today (from looking through `*/types.go` files), so this would be for library correctness currently.

**Untyped literal emission:** `toGolangSourceDataLiteral` uses `fmt.Sprintf("%v")` which emits untyped literals that may overflow or truncate near type boundaries.

### How to reproduce

1. Add `+k8s:minimum=-2147483649` to an `int32` field on a type with validation-gen enabled.
2. Run `hack/update-codegen.sh`.
3. Codegen succeeds, but the generated literal overflows `int32`.

Or:

1. Add `+k8s:maximum=18446744073709551615` to a `uint64` field.
2. Run `hack/update-codegen.sh`.
3. Codegen fails: `strconv.Atoi` cannot parse the value.

### What should happen

Codegen should reject values outside the target type's range with a clear error (e.g., "value -2147483649 does not fit in int32"). Values at the boundary (e.g., `-2147483648` for int32) should work correctly and emit properly typed Go literals.

### Fix outline

1. Split `ParseInt` into `ParseSignedInt` (returns `int64`) and `ParseUnsignedInt` (returns `uint64`).
2. In `minimumTagValidator`/`maximumTagValidator`, check the field's signedness, call the right parser, and validate the value fits the target type's range.
3. Emit typed literals (`int64(...)`, `uint64(...)`) in `toGolangSourceDataLiteral`.
4. Add boundary condition tests for int32 min/max, int64 min/max, uint32 max, uint64 max, and out-of-range rejection.

### Related PR // Background

Discovered via [PR #136589](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136589). @thockin noted from the PR there this issue as the PR there - specified +k8s:ifEnabled("WorkloadAwarePreemption")=+k8s:minimum=-2147483648 based on our guidance that all ints should spec min and max.

/sig api-machinery
/kind bug
pr_title: validation-gen: reject min/max tag values that overflow target integer type
pr_body:
#### What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

`validation-gen`'s `ParseInt` uses `strconv.Atoi`, which returns a platform-sized `int` and performs no validation against the target field's actual type. Writing `+k8s:minimum=-2147483649` on an `int32` field passes codegen without error but emits a literal that overflows `int32`, producing a Go compile failure with no indication that the tag value is out of range. For unsigned types, `strconv.Atoi` cannot parse values above `math.MaxInt64`, so valid `uint64` boundary values like `18446744073709551615` cannot be expressed at all.

This PR adds `ParseSignedInt` and `ParseUnsignedInt` to `util.go`, each accepting a `bitSize` parameter that validates the parsed value fits the target type's representable range at codegen time. The `minimumTagValidator` and `maximumTagValidator` in `limits.go` now resolve the field's bit width through a new `intBitSize` helper in `common.go`, select the signed or unsigned parser based on the gengo type, and store the result as `int64` or `uint64` in `FunctionGen.Args`.

The existing `ParseInt` function and all its other callers (maxLength, maxBytes, minItems, maxItems, equality, item, discriminator) are unchanged because those validators operate on lengths and counts that always fit in `int`.

The `toGolangSourceDataLiteral` switch at `validation.go:1518` already handles `int64` and `uint64` in its type list, so storing typed values in `FunctionGen.Args` requires no changes to the emission path.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Fixes #137965

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

No Kubernetes API types currently use unsigned integer fields, so the unsigned path is for library correctness. The signed path addresses the gap discovered via PR #136589 where `+k8s:minimum=-2147483648` was applied to an `int32` `Priority` field. That boundary value is valid, but it exposed that values one past the boundary (like `-2147483649`) would pass codegen silently and fail only at compile time.

**Testing done locally:**
- `go test ./staging/src/k8s.io/code-generator/cmd/validation-gen/...` passes all 120 test packages
- New `TestParseSignedInt` (29 cases) and `TestParseUnsignedInt` (17 cases) pass
- Boundary tests cover exact min/max for int8, int16, int32, int64, uint8, uint16, uint32, uint64
- Overflow by one in each direction, cross-boundary acceptance (value valid for int64 but not int32), canonical form rejection (leading zeros, unary plus, hex, octal, floating point, whitespace), and negative zero

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
NONE
```

#### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:

```docs
NONE
```

/sig api-machinery
/area api-validation

issue_title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
issue_body:
### What happened?

When operating in environments with high pod churn rates—where pods are frequently created and terminated—and multiple services are configured to reference a single deployment, we observed that network connectivity on some of the pods is failing on windows nodes.

On the pods where we have network issue, tried to do DNS lookup by exec into the pods and it failed to resolve the DNS.

Attached are the logs on two windows nodes

[9a98.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25727718/9a98.zip)

[bf83.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25727725/bf83.zip)

### What did you expect to happen?

Network connectivity shouldn't break on windows pods and DNS resolution should succeed.


### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1.  Deploy kubernetes cluster with limited available IPs for pods on both Linux and Windows node.
2. Deploy Linux pods that consume almost all IPs.
3. Deploy service type LoadBalancer that refer to Linux pods, This will trigger kube-proxy to create Remote HNS endpoint on Windows. This Remote HNS endpoint will have Linux pod IP.
4. Scale down the Linux pods to 1.
5. Do multiple time scaling up and down.
6. Deploy windows deployment with multiple replicas and have multiple service referencing the deployment.
7. Scale down the windows pods to 1.
8. Perform the same operation multiple time (scaling up and down)
9. After few days we see that on few of the pods, if we do the nslookup, it fails to connect and resolve DNS.

### Anything else we need to know?

I was going through the below PR and want to check whether any regression caused the network connectivity issue. 
PR1: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135146
PR2: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/133953
All our customers reported that, they are seeing intermittent network connectivity issues on K8S >1.30. 


### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
# paste output here
1.31 and higher versions
```

</details>


### Cloud provider

<details>
EKS


### OS version

<details>

```console
# On Linux:
$ cat /etc/os-release
# paste output here
$ uname -a
# paste output here

# On Windows:
C:\> wmic os get Caption, Version, BuildNumber, OSArchitecture
# paste output here
BuildNumber  Caption                                   OSArchitecture  Version
20348        Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Datacenter  64-bit          10.0.20348
```

</details>

pr_title: Delete remote endpoint if it has same ip as local endpoint in the system.
pr_body:
#### What type of PR is this?
/kind bug

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

**Handling Stale Remote Endpoints When Local and Remote Endpoints Share the Same IP (getAllEndpointsByNetwork)**

On Windows Server 2019 and Windows Server 2022, within an L2Bridge network configuration, all pods across cluster nodes typically share the same pod subnet.

In scenarios where the subnet range is limited, or even when larger ranges are heavily utilized, IP address reuse can occur. For example, when a pod assigned an IP address (e.g., 10.0.0.1) is deleted, that IP may later be reassigned to a new pod on a different node immediately. In such cases, the CNI creates a new local endpoint for the reused IP.

However, the corresponding remote endpoint associated with the previously deleted pod may not yet have been cleaned up, as it could still be awaiting deletion signals. Because the IP is already reassigned locally, KubeProxy may inadvertently increase the reference count of the stale remote endpoint and delay its removal. This can result in both a local and a remote endpoint coexisting with the same IP address in HNS.

This duplication can lead to unintended behavior. For instance, when a pod using the reused IP attempts DNS resolution, traffic is load-balanced at the host port (particularly in non-DSR mode). After DNAT, the VFP rule may select the MAC address of the remote endpoint instead of the local pod. Since subsequent VFP processing interprets this as traffic destined for a remote endpoint, packets may be encapsulated and routed to an incorrect node rather than the appropriate DNS pod. This can ultimately cause DNS timeouts.

To address this issue, the fix prioritizes local endpoints. When a local endpoint is detected for an IP that also has a remote endpoint, the remote endpoint is marked for deletion and replaced by the local one. Additionally, any stale remote endpoints with duplicate IP addresses are systematically cleaned up using deleteAllRemoteEndpointsWithDupIP at the end of each syncProxyRules() cycle.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
Fixes #137381 

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
Fixed stale remote HNS endpoint cleanup on Windows when a pod IP is reused across nodes in L2Bridge networks, preventing DNS timeouts caused by traffic being routed to the wrong node.
```

#### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:

```docs

```
/sig windows
/sig network
/area kube-proxy
issue_text:
Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30 ### What happened?

When operating in environments with high pod churn rates—where pods are frequently created and terminated—and multiple services are configured to reference a single deployment, we observed that network connectivity on some of the pods is failing on windows nodes.

On the pods where we have network issue, tried to do DNS lookup by exec into the pods and it failed to resolve the DNS.

Attached are the logs on two windows nodes

[9a98.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25727718/9a98.zip)

[bf83.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25727725/bf83.zip)

### What did you expect to happen?

Network connectivity shouldn't break on windows pods and DNS resolution should succeed.


### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1.  Deploy kubernetes cluster with limited available IPs for pods on both Linux and Windows node.
2. Deploy Linux pods that consume almost all IPs.
3. Deploy service type LoadBalancer that refer to Linux pods, This will trigger kube-proxy to create Remote HNS endpoint on Windows. This Remote HNS endpoint will have Linux pod IP.
4. Scale down the Linux pods to 1.
5. Do multiple time scaling up and down.
6. Deploy windows deployment with multiple replicas and have multiple service referencing the deployment.
7. Scale down the windows pods to 1.
8. Perform the same operation multiple time (scaling up and down)
9. After few days we see that on few of the pods, if we do the nslookup, it fails to connect and resolve DNS.

### Anything else we need to know?

I was going through the below PR and want to check whether any regression caused the network connectivity issue. 
PR1: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135146
PR2: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/133953
All our customers reported that, they are seeing intermittent network connectivity issues on K8S >1.30.

$ kubectl version
# paste output here
1.31 and higher versions
```

issue_title: The Clientset returned from the new NewClientset function does not work for CRDs
issue_body:
### What happened?

Calling the `Create` method fails with, eg

`failed to convert new object (/cluster-egressIP; submariner.io/v1, Kind=ClusterGlobalEgressIP) to smd typed: schema error: no type found matching: com.github.submariner-io.submariner.pkg.apis.submariner.io.v1.ClusterGlobalEgressIP`

Long story short, It ends up trying to validate a resource using the global `schemaYAML` var defined in the generated file _pkg/client/applyconfiguration/internal/internal.go_. However this is not populated with the appropriate types. So `NewClientset` is unusable even though `NewSimpleClientset` is now marked as deprecated.

Note that `NewClientset` works fine with core K8s types because its [schemaYAML](https://github.com/kubernetes/client-go/blob/46965213e4561ad1b9c585d1c3551a0cc8d3fcd6/applyconfigurations/internal/internal.go#L41) is populated appropriately. However [apiextensions](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/243fdafc6824d6b33faa081efec2ebc4cb164010/staging/src/k8s.io/apiextensions-apiserver/pkg/client/applyconfiguration/internal/internal.go#L41) is not.

### What did you expect to happen?

I expect the `Clientset` methods to work for CRDs. It seems the code generator needs to be updated.

Also `NewSimpleClientset` should not be deprecated unless/until `NewClientset` works.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

```
import fakeclientset "k8s.io/apiextensions-apiserver/pkg/client/clientset/clientset/fake"
...
client := fakeclientset.NewClientset()
client.ApiextensionsV1().CustomResourceDefinitions().Create(ctx, crd, metav1.CreateOptions{})
```

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
# paste output here
```

pr_title: fake client-go: un-deprecate NewSimpleClientset
pr_body:
#### What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

NewSimpleClientset was marked as deprecated when NewClientset was introduced. This has caused some confusion:
- Not all packages have NewClientset (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/135980).
- Tests that work with NewSimpleClientset fail when switched to NewClientset (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136327) because of missing CRD support (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/126850).

It doesn't seem burdensome to keep NewSimpleClientset around forever. Some unit tests may even prefer to use it when they don't need server-side apply (less overhead). Therefore there is no need to deprecate it.

This avoids churn in the eco system because contributors no longer create PRs "because the linter complains about the usage of a deprecated function".

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/135980, https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136327, https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/126850 and probably more.

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
```release-note
NONE
```

/cc @soltysh @jpbetz @aojea

issue_title: [kube-api-linter] Enable the `optionalorrequired` linter for the `resource` API group
issue_body:
As part of the work outlined in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/134671 to enable existing kube-api-linter rules, the `optionalorrequired` linter needs to be enabled for the `resource` API group. There are 16 existing issues that will need to be addressed when enabling the linter for this api group.

See https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/134675 as an example of enabling the `optionalorrequired` linter for an API group.

/sig architecture 
/sig api-machinery
pr_title: api: enable optionalorrequired linter for resource API
pr_body:
#### What type of PR is this?
/kind cleanup
<!--
Add one of the following kinds:
/kind bug
/kind dependency
/kind cleanup
/kind documentation
/kind feature

Optionally add one or more of the following kinds if applicable:
/kind api-change
/kind deprecation
/kind failing-test
/kind flake
/kind regression
-->

#### What this PR does / why we need it:
Add +optional marker to various specs in the resource API to satisfy the optionalorrequired linter rule and enable the linter for the resource API group.

Since `nonpointerstructs` is now enabled, these fields should be optional given that they all contain no required fields.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
Fixes https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136873
<!--
Please link relevant issues to help with tracking.

issue_title: job executions started manually for a cronjob are marked as unexpected
issue_body:
### What happened?

We have a custom resource with a name - `Foo`
We have a CronJob named - `Foo`

Now, we have a job whose owner refernce is set to the custom resource name - `Foo`. We have noticed that the job when stopped (suspended) basically causes an event/warning - "[UnexpectedJob](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/8b132ea40a250e9acf08ab2fadb9a8c2fd811d74/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L444C39-L444C55)" for the given job execution.

[syncCronJob](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/8b132ea40a250e9acf08ab2fadb9a8c2fd811d74/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L422C25-L422C36) is trying to reconcile the job because it thinks that it is owned by the CronJob. Probably because of "[getJobsToBeReconciled](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/8b132ea40a250e9acf08ab2fadb9a8c2fd811d74/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L252C25-L252C46)", at this [line](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/8b132ea40a250e9acf08ab2fadb9a8c2fd811d74/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L264C3-L264C110).

### What did you expect to happen?

The `syncCronJob` should not try to reconcile the job because it is not owned by the CronJob. 

**Proposed Solution**: [syncCronJob](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/8b132ea40a250e9acf08ab2fadb9a8c2fd811d74/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L422C25-L422C36) should not reconcile the job, since it is owned by the CronJob. The `getJobsToBeReconciled` method should match the `name` and the `kind` of the job. The `kind` in the case of a cronjob would be `CronJob`.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

- Run a job execution using the same CRD used to create a cronjob.
- Once the job execution starts, stop (suspend) it.
- We would start getting events of type `Warning` with reason - `UnexpectedJob`.
- Stop a job execution that was triggered due to the cronjob itself and we would not see warnings for this stopped job execution.

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

issue_title: [Umbrella] KEP-5832 / KEP-4671: Decouple PodGroup API & Workload API Alpha2 for v1.36
issue_body:
This issue tracks the implementation work for v1.36 spanning two related KEPs:

- KEP-5832: Decouple PodGroup API from Workload ([k/enhancements KEP](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-scheduling/5832-decouple-podgroup-api))
- KEP-4671: Gang Scheduling Workload API v1alpha2 updates ([k/enhancements KEP](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-scheduling/4671-gang-scheduling))

**Work Items**
- [x] API Changes (#136976)
    - Create standalone PodGroup API 
    - Update Pod API
    - Refactor Workload spec , API version change (v1alpha1 → v1alpha2)
 - [x] Add declarative validation for `PodGroup` resource and update the `Workload` validation (#135164)
- [x] Add admission plugin that verifies Workload referenced by a PodGroup exists (#137464)
- [x] Add protection controller for PodGroup (#137641)
- [x] PodGroup status updates by kube-scheduler (#137611)
- [x] Scheduler Changes (#136976)
    - Update WorkloadManager updates
    - Update GangScheduling plugin for new API shape and add PodGroup Informer
- [x] Testing (#136976)
    - Update existing integration tests for decoupled API shape
    - Add integration tests for PodGroup lifecycle independent of Workload
    - Add e2e coverage for PodGroup creation and reference from Workload

/kind feature
/sig scheduling
pr_title: Create Workload API v1alpha2
pr_body:
#### What type of PR is this?
/kind feature
/kind api-change

#### What this PR does / why we need it:
This PR creates v1alpha2 version of the Workload API (and, at the same time, deletes the old version of that API, v1alpha1) and introduces a brand new v1alpha2 PodGroup API. It also replaces WorkloadRef field in the Pod API with the SchedulingGroup field that refers to the new PodGroup API.

This is basically a lion share of the work related to decoupling the PodGroups from Workloads as a separate API which is described in KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5832.

#### Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/4671
KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5832

#### Special notes for your reviewer:
/sig scheduling

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
```release-note
Introduced scheduling.k8s.io/v1alpha2 Workload and PodGroup API to allow for expressing workload-level scheduling requirements and let kube-scheduler act on those. Removed scheduling.k8s.io/v1alpha1 Workload API.
```

#### Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:
```docs
- [KEP]: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/4671
- [KEP]: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5832
```

combined_text:
Tweak irrefutable let else warning output Given
```rust
pub fn say_hello(name: Option<String>) {
    let name_str = Some(name) else {
        println!("Hello person!");
        return;
    };

    // TODO: greet the user by name.
}
```
after https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/152938 [the output is](https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=nightly&mode=debug&edition=2024&gist=0371c0c7bc36f532af41281232081aaf)

```
warning: irrefutable `let...else` pattern
 --> src/lib.rs:2:5
  |
2 |     let name_str = Some(name) else {
  |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  |
  = note: this pattern always matches, so the else clause is unreachable
help: remove this `else` block
 --> src/lib.rs:2:36
  |
2 |       let name_str = Some(name) else {
  |  ____________________________________^
3 | |         println!("Hello person!");
4 | |         return;
5 | |     };
  | |_____^
  = note: `#[warn(irrefutable_let_patterns)]` on by default
```

It could do with some tweaks. We can provide a structured suggestion (not very convinced that that is the right suggestion unless we have a lot of certainty about the types involved) and instead of a note about the irrefutable pattern, make it a span label:

```
warning: unreachable `else` clause
 --> src/lib.rs:2:5
  |
2 |     let name_str = Some(name) else {
  |         --------              ^^^^
  |         |
  |         assigning to binding pattern `name_str` will always succeed
  = note: `#[warn(irrefutable_let_patterns)]` on by default
help: consider using `let Some(name_str) = name` to match on a specific variant
  |
2 -     let name_str = Some(name) else {
2 +     let Some(name_str) = Some(name) else {
  |
```

combined_text:
Support `__mulsi3` for m68k I tried to build a simple example with `alloc` by [following the instructions for m68k-unknown-none-elf](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/rustc/platform-support/m68k-unknown-none-elf.html). Linking it fails: `error: linking with m68k-linux-gnu-ld failed: exit status: 1` .. `undefined reference to __mulsi3`

I tried this code:

main.rs:
```rust
#![no_std]
#![no_main]

extern crate alloc;
use alloc::alloc::{GlobalAlloc, Layout};
use alloc::vec::Vec;

pub struct DummyAllocator;
unsafe impl GlobalAlloc for DummyAllocator {
    unsafe fn alloc(&self, _layout: Layout) -> *mut u8 {
        core::ptr::null_mut() // just for testing
    }
    unsafe fn dealloc(&self, _ptr: *mut u8, _layout: Layout) {
        panic!("dealloc should be never called")
    }
}

#[global_allocator]
static ALLOCATOR: DummyAllocator = DummyAllocator;

#[unsafe(no_mangle)]
extern "C" fn _start() {
    let mut v = Vec::new();
    v.push(1);
}

#[panic_handler]
fn panic(_info: &core::panic::PanicInfo) -> ! {
    loop {}
}
```
Cargo.toml:
```toml
[package]
name = "hello-alloc"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"
publish = false
```
.cargo/config.toml (using the [recommended linker](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/rustc/platform-support/m68k-unknown-none-elf.html)):
```toml
[unstable]
build-std = ["panic_abort", "core", "alloc"]

[target.m68k-unknown-none-elf]
linker = "m68k-linux-gnu-ld"
```
Build with:
```sh
cargo +nightly build --release --target m68k-unknown-none-elf
```
If I copy `__mulsi3` from [riscv](https://github.com/rust-lang/compiler-builtins/blob/85db9f1923007c287b894d392b880545ec70d05e/compiler-builtins/src/riscv.rs#L20-L34) to `main.rs`, the code can be built.

Could `__mulsi3` be copied from riscv to m68k [compiler-builtins](https://github.com/rust-lang/compiler-builtins) so that I don't have to implement it myself?

I don't know if `__muldi3` should also be copied from riscv to m68k. I made a simple test multiplying two `u64` values and it seemed to build fine without adding `__muldi3` to `main.rs`. The resulting code had an implementation for `__muldi3` which was calling `__mulsi3`.

### Meta

`rustc --version --verbose`:
```
rustc +nightly --version --verbose
rustc 1.96.0-nightly (2d76d9bc7 2026-03-09)
binary: rustc
commit-hash: 2d76d9bc76f27b03b4899e72ce561c7ac2c5cf6b
commit-date: 2026-03-09
host: aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu
release: 1.96.0-nightly
LLVM version: 22.1.0
```

Created using https://github.com/rust-lang/josh-sync.

cc @tgross35 if this is not a good time to sync, but it looks like it's been a while and this should specifically close https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/153772

pr_title: Uplift `clippy::cmp_nan` lint
pr_body:
This PR aims at uplifting the `clippy::cmp_nan` lint into rustc.

## `invalid_nan_comparisons`

~~(deny-by-default)~~ (warn-by-default)

The `invalid_nan_comparisons` lint checks comparison with `f32::NAN` or `f64::NAN` as one of the operand.

### Example

```rust,compile_fail
let a = 2.3f32;
if a == f32::NAN {}
```

### Explanation

NaN does not compare meaningfully to anything – not even itself – so those comparisons are always false.

-----

Mostly followed the instructions for uplifting a clippy lint described here: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/99696#pullrequestreview-1134072751

@rustbot label: +I-lang-nominated
r? compiler

issue_body:

I expected to see this happen: compiles Instead, this happened: It fails to compile core: ``` rustc: /home/manuel/prog/rust_gpu/src/llvm-project/llvm/lib/CodeGen/SelectionDAG/SelectionDAG.cpp:9502: SDValue llvm::SelectionDAG::getLoad(ISD::MemIndexedMode, ISD::LoadExtType, EVT, const SDLoc &, SDValue, SDValue, SDValue, EVT, MachineMemOperand *): Assertion `(!MMO->getRanges() || (mdconst::extract(MMO->getRanges()->getOperand(0)) ->getBitWidth() == MemVT.getScalarSizeInBits() && MemVT.isInteger())) && "Range metadata and load type must match!"' failed. ``` This is *not* reproducible on nightly, which doesn't seem surprising given: https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/backend/debugging.html?highlight=llvm-assertions#enable-llvm-internal-checks ### Meta `rustc --version --verbose`: ``` latest master, build from source ``` cc @Flakebi
Backtrace

``` ```
pr_title: Offload intrinsic
pr_body:
<!-- homu-ignore:start -->
<!--
If this PR is related to an unstable feature or an otherwise tracked effort,
please link to the relevant tracking issue here. If you don't know of a related
tracking issue or there are none, feel free to ignore this.

This PR will get automatically assigned to a reviewer. In case you would like
a specific user to review your work, you can assign it to them by using

    r? <reviewer name>
-->
<!-- homu-ignore:end -->

This PR implements the minimal mechanisms required to run a small subset of arbitrary offload kernels without relying on hardcoded names or metadata.

- `offload(kernel, (..args))`: an intrinsic that generates the necessary host-side LLVM-IR code.
- `rustc_offload_kernel`: a builtin attribute that marks device kernels to be handled appropriately.

Example usage (pseudocode):
```rust
fn kernel(x: *mut [f64; 128]) {
    core::intrinsics::offload(kernel_1, (x,))
}

#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
extern "C" {
    pub fn kernel_1(array_b: *mut [f64; 128]);
}

#[cfg(not(target_os = "linux"))]
#[rustc_offload_kernel]
extern "gpu-kernel" fn kernel_1(x: *mut [f64; 128]) {
    unsafe { (*x)[0] = 21.0 };
}
```

pr_title: Uplift `clippy::cmp_nan` lint
pr_body:
This PR aims at uplifting the `clippy::cmp_nan` lint into rustc.

## `invalid_nan_comparisons`

~~(deny-by-default)~~ (warn-by-default)

The `invalid_nan_comparisons` lint checks comparison with `f32::NAN` or `f64::NAN` as one of the operand.

### Example

```rust,compile_fail
let a = 2.3f32;
if a == f32::NAN {}
```

### Explanation

NaN does not compare meaningfully to anything – not even itself – so those comparisons are always false.

-----

Mostly followed the instructions for uplifting a clippy lint described here: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/99696#pullrequestreview-1134072751

@rustbot label: +I-lang-nominated
r? compiler

Uplift `clippy::cmp_nan` lint This PR aims at uplifting the `clippy::cmp_nan` lint into rustc.

## `invalid_nan_comparisons`

~~(deny-by-default)~~ (warn-by-default)

The `invalid_nan_comparisons` lint checks comparison with `f32::NAN` or `f64::NAN` as one of the operand.

### Example

```rust,compile_fail
let a = 2.3f32;
if a == f32::NAN {}
```

### Explanation

NaN does not compare meaningfully to anything – not even itself – so those comparisons are always false.

-----

Mostly followed the instructions for uplifting a clippy lint described here: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/99696#pullrequestreview-1134072751

issue_title: propagate constraints during copying
pr_title: Allow foreign exceptions to unwind through Rust code and Rust panics to unwind through FFI
pr_body:
This PR fixes interactions between Rust panics and foreign (mainly C++) exceptions.

C++ exceptions (and other FFI exceptions) can now safely unwind through Rust code:
- The FFI function causing the unwind must be marked with `#[unwind(allowed)]`. If this is not the case then LLVM may optimize landing pads away with the assumption that they are unreachable.
- Drop code will be executed as the exception unwinds through the stack, as with a Rust panic.
- `catch_unwind` will *not* catch the exception, instead the exception will silently continue unwinding past it.

Rust panics can now safely unwind through C++ code:
- C++ destructors will be called as the stack unwinds.
- The Rust panic can only be caught with `catch (...)`, after which it can be either rethrown or discarded.
- C++ cannot name the type of the Rust exception object used for unwinding, which means that it can't be caught explicitly or have its contents inspected.

Tests have been added to ensure all of the above works correctly.

Some notes about non-C++ exceptions:
- `pthread_cancel` and `pthread_exit` use unwinding on glibc. This has the same behavior as a C++ exception: destructors are run but it cannot be caught by `catch_unwind`.
- `longjmp` on Windows is implemented using unwinding. Destructors are run on MSVC, but not on MinGW. In both cases the unwind cannot be caught by `catch_unwind`.
- As with C++ exceptions, you need to mark the relevant FFI functions with `#[unwind(allowed)]`, otherwise LLVM will optimize out the destructors since they seem unreachable.

I haven't updated any of the documentation, so officially unwinding through FFI is still UB. However this is a step towards making it well-defined.

Fixes #65441

cc @gnzlbg
r? @alexcrichton

combined_text:
propagate constraints during copying Copying from one slot to another should add a copy of every constraint that
includes the RHS of the assignment to the copy postcondition (with the RHS
renamed, in the constraint, to the new LHS slot).

In other words:

  let int x = 10;
  let int y = 11;
  check lt(x,y);    // postcond = { lt(x,y) }
  let int z = x;    // postcond = { lt(x,y), lt(z,y) }

Unless some horrible complications arise that make this impossible, I believe
the current rules permit this propagation to work.

- C++ destructors will be called as the stack unwinds.
- The Rust panic can only be caught with `catch (...)`, after which it can be either rethrown or discarded.
- C++ cannot name the type of the Rust exception object used for unwinding, which means that it can't be caught explicitly or have its contents inspected.

Tests have been added to ensure all of the above works correctly.

Some notes about non-C++ exceptions:
- `pthread_cancel` and `pthread_exit` use unwinding on glibc. This has the same behavior as a C++ exception: destructors are run but it cannot be caught by `catch_unwind`.
- `longjmp` on Windows is implemented using unwinding. Destructors are run on MSVC, but not on MinGW. In both cases the unwind cannot be caught by `catch_unwind`.
- As with C++ exceptions, you need to mark the relevant FFI functions with `#[unwind(allowed)]`, otherwise LLVM will optimize out the destructors since they seem unreachable.

I haven't updated any of the documentation, so officially unwinding through FFI is still UB. However this is a step towards making it well-defined.

Fixes #65441

issue_title: Redundant clone suggestion in move error
issue_body:
We currently emit two suggestions to `.clone()` in some move errors:

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/35f1109ece895199e4b8d755b101e9a481d18243/tests/ui/suggestions/option-content-move.stderr#L12-L19

We should emit only one of the two.

pr_title: Avoid redundant clone suggestions in borrowck diagnostics
pr_body:
<!-- homu-ignore:start -->
*[View all comments](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-comments/rust-lang/rust/pull/154197)*
<!-- homu-ignore:end -->

Fixes rust-lang/rust#153886

Removed redundant `.clone()` suggestions.

I found that there are two patterns to handle this issue while I was implementing:

- Should suggest only UFCS
- Should suggest only simple `.clone()`

For the target issue, we can just remove the UFCS (`<Option<String> as Clone>::clone(&selection.1)`) side.

However, for the `BorrowedContentSource::OverloadedDeref` pattern like `Rc<Vec<i32>>`, for instance the `borrowck-move-out-of-overloaded-auto-deref.rs` test case, I think we need to employ the UFCS way. The actual test case is:

```rust
//@ run-rustfix
use std::rc::Rc;

pub fn main() {
    let _x = Rc::new(vec![1, 2]).into_iter();
    //~^ ERROR [E0507]
}
```

And another error will be shown if we simply use the simple `.clone()` pattern. Like:

```rust
use std::rc::Rc;

pub fn main() {
    let _x = Rc::new(vec![1, 2]).clone().into_iter();
}
```

then we will get

```
error[E0507]: cannot move out of an `Rc`
   --> src/main.rs:5:14
    |
  5 |     let _x = Rc::new(vec![1, 2]).clone().into_iter();
    |              ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ ----------- value moved due to this method call
    |              |
    |              move occurs because value has type `Vec<i32>`, which does not implement the `Copy` trait
    |
note: `into_iter` takes ownership of the receiver `self`, which moves value
   --> /playground/.rustup/toolchains/stable-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/lib/rustlib/src/rust/library/core/src/iter/traits/collect.rs:310:18
    |
310 |     fn into_iter(self) -> Self::IntoIter;
    |                  ^^^^
help: you can `clone` the value and consume it, but this might not be your desired behavior
    |
  5 -     let _x = Rc::new(vec![1, 2]).clone().into_iter();
  5 +     let _x = <Vec<i32> as Clone>::clone(&Rc::new(vec![1, 2])).into_iter();
    |

For more information about this error, try `rustc --explain E0507`.
```

[Rust Playground](https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2024&gist=7e767bed3f1c573c03642f20f454ed03)

In this case, `Rc::clone` only increments the reference count and returns a new `Rc<Vec<i32>>`; it does not grant ownership of the inner `Vec<i32>`. As a result, calling into_iter() attempts to move the `Vec<i32>`, leading to the same E0507 error again.

On the other hand, in UFCS form:

```
<Vec<i32> as Clone>::clone(&Rc::new(vec![1, 2])).into_iter()
```

This explicitly calls `<Vec<i32> as Clone>::clone`, and the argument `&Rc<Vec<i32>>` is treated as `&Vec<i32>` via Rc’s `Deref` implementation. As a result, the `Vec<i32>` itself is cloned, yielding an owned `Vec<i32>`, which allows `into_iter()` to succeed, if my understanding is correct.

I addressed the issue as far as I could find the edge cases but please advice me if I'm overlooking something.

issue_title: ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable? does not work with composite keys
issue_body:
### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  # Activate the gem you are reporting the issue against.
  gem "activerecord", "~> 8.1" # Also happens on 7.2 (didn't test earlier as Trilogy wasn't available)
  gem "trilogy"
  gem "debug"
end

require "active_record"
require "logger"

module ActiveRecordCompositeKeyInversePatch
  private

  def matches_foreign_key?(record)
    fk = reflection.foreign_key

    unless fk.is_a?(Array)
      return super
    end

    pk = Array(reflection.association_primary_key)

    owner_fk_values = fk.map { |key| owner._read_attribute(key) }
    record_pk_values = pk.map { |key| record._read_attribute(key) }

    owner_fk_values == record_pk_values
  end
end

# Patching with the above module fixes the issue as composite keys
# ActiveRecord::Associations::Association.prepend(ActiveRecordCompositeKeyInversePatch)

logger = Logger.new(STDOUT)
ActiveRecord::Base.logger = logger

ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(
  adapter: "trilogy",
  database: "test",
  host: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_HOST"),
  port: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_PORT"),
  password: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_PASSWORD"),
  username: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_USER")
)

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :parents, force: true, primary_key: [:store_id, :id] do |t|
    t.integer :store_id, null: false
    t.integer :id, null: false
    t.string :last_name
  end

  create_table :children, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :store_id, null: false
    t.integer :parent_id, null: false
  end
end

class Parent < ActiveRecord::Base
  self.primary_key = :id

  has_many :children, inverse_of: :parent, foreign_key: :parent_id, primary_key: :id

  query_constraints :store_id, :id

  validates :id, presence: true
  validates :store_id, presence: true
end

class Child < ActiveRecord::Base
  belongs_to :parent, inverse_of: :children, foreign_key: [:store_id, :parent_id], primary_key: [:store_id, :id]

  validates :parent_id, presence: true
  validates :store_id, presence: true
end

parent = Parent.new
parent.store_id = 1
parent.id = 1
parent.last_name = "Smith"
parent.save!
parent.reload
logger.info("Parent: #{parent.to_json}")

child = Child.new
child.store_id = parent.store_id
child.parent_id = parent.id
child.save!

child = Child.first!
logger.info("Parent object_id: #{child.parent.object_id}")
logger.info("Navigation parent object_id: #{child.parent.children.first.parent.object_id}")
unless child.parent.object_id == child.parent.children.first.parent.object_id
  logger.error "inverse_of isn't set on child.parent.children.first.parent"
end

```

### Expected behavior

The `ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable?` method works with composite keys, resulting in re-using the already loaded object instance instead of doing a new query and instantiating a new object.

The following queries would be executed:
```
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.505020 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.507674 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.4ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.507980 #53610]  INFO -- : Parent object_id: 1960
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.509779 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.3ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.509846 #53610]  INFO -- : Navigation parent object_id: 1960
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.510247 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.3ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
```

### Actual behavior

The `ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable?` method calls `matches_foreign_key?(record)`, but `matches_foreign_key?(record)` doesn't take into account composite keys:

```ruby
        def inversable?(record)
          record &&
            ((!record.persisted? || !owner.persisted?) || matches_foreign_key?(record))
        end

        def matches_foreign_key?(record)
          if foreign_key_for?(record)
            record.read_attribute(reflection.foreign_key) == owner.id ||
              (foreign_key_for?(owner) && owner.read_attribute(reflection.foreign_key) == record.id)
          else
            owner.read_attribute(reflection.foreign_key) == record.id
          end
        end
```

`record.read_attribute` ends up receiving `["store_id", "parent_id"]` which doesn't exist and returns `nil`:
```ruby
(rdbg) fk
=> ["store_id", "parent_id"]
(ruby) record.read_attribute(fk)
=> nil
```


ActiveRecord fails to determine the relationship is `inversable?`, and ends up triggering a new query for the Parent, and loads a new instance:
```
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.173841 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.177039 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.4ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1

pr_title: Fix inverse_of on composite keys in ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable?
pr_body:
### Motivation / Background

Fixes https://github.com/rails/rails/issues/56662

The issue posted a simple replication script, including a monkeypatch that would address the issue. This PR refactors that patch into an integral part of the `ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#matches_foreign_key?` method.

### Detail

Composite keys would fail on the `record.read_attribute` and `record._has_attribute?` checks as this would pass in an array to a method expecting a string.

Each key component needs to be read one-by-one.

### Checklist

Before submitting the PR make sure the following are checked:

* [x] This Pull Request is related to one change. Unrelated changes should be opened in separate PRs.

* [x] Tests are added or updated if you fix a bug or add a feature.
* [x] CHANGELOG files are updated for the changed libraries if there is a behavior change or additional feature. Minor bug fixes and documentation changes should not be included.
issue_text:
ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable? does not work with composite keys ### Steps to reproduce

```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

require "bundler/inline"

gemfile(true) do
  source "https://rubygems.org"

  # Activate the gem you are reporting the issue against.
  gem "activerecord", "~> 8.1" # Also happens on 7.2 (didn't test earlier as Trilogy wasn't available)
  gem "trilogy"
  gem "debug"
end

require "active_record"
require "logger"

module ActiveRecordCompositeKeyInversePatch
  private

  def matches_foreign_key?(record)
    fk = reflection.foreign_key

    unless fk.is_a?(Array)
      return super
    end

    pk = Array(reflection.association_primary_key)

    owner_fk_values = fk.map { |key| owner._read_attribute(key) }
    record_pk_values = pk.map { |key| record._read_attribute(key) }

    owner_fk_values == record_pk_values
  end
end

# Patching with the above module fixes the issue as composite keys
# ActiveRecord::Associations::Association.prepend(ActiveRecordCompositeKeyInversePatch)

logger = Logger.new(STDOUT)
ActiveRecord::Base.logger = logger

ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection(
  adapter: "trilogy",
  database: "test",
  host: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_HOST"),
  port: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_PORT"),
  password: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_PASSWORD"),
  username: ENV.fetch("MYSQL_USER")
)

ActiveRecord::Schema.define do
  create_table :parents, force: true, primary_key: [:store_id, :id] do |t|
    t.integer :store_id, null: false
    t.integer :id, null: false
    t.string :last_name
  end

  create_table :children, force: true do |t|
    t.integer :store_id, null: false
    t.integer :parent_id, null: false
  end
end

class Parent < ActiveRecord::Base
  self.primary_key = :id

  has_many :children, inverse_of: :parent, foreign_key: :parent_id, primary_key: :id

  query_constraints :store_id, :id

  validates :id, presence: true
  validates :store_id, presence: true
end

class Child < ActiveRecord::Base
  belongs_to :parent, inverse_of: :children, foreign_key: [:store_id, :parent_id], primary_key: [:store_id, :id]

  validates :parent_id, presence: true
  validates :store_id, presence: true
end

parent = Parent.new
parent.store_id = 1
parent.id = 1
parent.last_name = "Smith"
parent.save!
parent.reload
logger.info("Parent: #{parent.to_json}")

child = Child.new
child.store_id = parent.store_id
child.parent_id = parent.id
child.save!

child = Child.first!
logger.info("Parent object_id: #{child.parent.object_id}")
logger.info("Navigation parent object_id: #{child.parent.children.first.parent.object_id}")
unless child.parent.object_id == child.parent.children.first.parent.object_id
  logger.error "inverse_of isn't set on child.parent.children.first.parent"
end

```

### Expected behavior

The `ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable?` method works with composite keys, resulting in re-using the already loaded object instance instead of doing a new query and instantiating a new object.

The following queries would be executed:
```
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.505020 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.507674 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.4ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.507980 #53610]  INFO -- : Parent object_id: 1960
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.509779 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.3ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.509846 #53610]  INFO -- : Navigation parent object_id: 1960
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:49.510247 #53610] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.3ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
```

### Actual behavior

The `ActiveRecord::Associations::Association#inversable?` method calls `matches_foreign_key?(record)`, but `matches_foreign_key?(record)` doesn't take into account composite keys:

```ruby
        def inversable?(record)
          record &&
            ((!record.persisted? || !owner.persisted?) || matches_foreign_key?(record))
        end

        def matches_foreign_key?(record)
          if foreign_key_for?(record)
            record.read_attribute(reflection.foreign_key) == owner.id ||
              (foreign_key_for?(owner) && owner.read_attribute(reflection.foreign_key) == record.id)
          else
            owner.read_attribute(reflection.foreign_key) == record.id
          end
        end
```

`record.read_attribute` ends up receiving `["store_id", "parent_id"]` which doesn't exist and returns `nil`:
```ruby
(rdbg) fk
=> ["store_id", "parent_id"]
(ruby) record.read_attribute(fk)
=> nil
```


ActiveRecord fails to determine the relationship is `inversable?`, and ends up triggering a new query for the Parent, and loads a new instance:
```
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.173841 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.177039 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.4ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.177438 #53687]  INFO -- : Parent object_id: 1960
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.179559 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180296 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.2ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
I, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180342 #53687]  INFO -- : Navigation parent object_id: 1968
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180618 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Child Load (0.1ms)  SELECT `children`.* FROM `children` WHERE `children`.`parent_id` = 1 ORDER BY `children`.`id` ASC LIMIT 1
D, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180886 #53687] DEBUG -- :   Parent Load (0.1ms)  SELECT `parents`.* FROM `parents` WHERE `parents`.`store_id` = 1 AND `parents`.`id` = 1 LIMIT 1
E, [2026-01-22T12:31:58.180913 #53687] ERROR -- : inverse_of isn't set on child.parent.children.first.parent
```

Interestingly, `ActiveRecord::Associations::Preloader.new(records: [parent], associations: [:children]).call` makes it work properly.

### System configuration

**Rails version**: 7 and 8 (latest in rubygems)

**Ruby version**:  3.4.7
